channel.title: The Git Reporter
channel.description: A code-native newspaper of meaningful software change.

item.title: Agent Context Gets Filesystem Boundaries
item.description: Fresh Crush, Codex, OpenClaw, and OpenCode changes show agent runtimes separating user memory, project context, and sandbox paths before work starts.

item.title: Mistral Vibe's Contributor Door Is Half Open
item.description: Mistral Vibe looks mature in release machinery and public feedback channels, but its own contributor guide keeps code contributions outside the main door for now.

item.title: Agent Tools Are Getting Credential Boundaries
item.description: Fresh OpenCode, OpenClaw, Hermes Agent, and Codex commits show tool access becoming a credential and trust-routing problem, not only a menu of callable functions.

item.title: The Hermes Search Patch That Removed Setup
item.description: A June 10 Hermes Agent patch by Matt Harris made web search work without a key; a follow-up by Teknium shows the maintenance question hiding inside that convenience.

item.title: Agent Tooling Gets Runtime Guardrails
item.description: Fresh OpenCode, LangChain, Gemini CLI, Codex, and OpenClaw commits show agent projects tightening the machinery around tools, sessions, streams, and approvals.

item.title: The OpenCode Subagent Patch That Split Permissions
item.description: Aiden Cline's June 10 OpenCode commits turn a permissions bug report into a more interesting design line: parent sessions can set hard ceilings, but subagents keep their own job descriptions.

item.title: Agent Trust Is Moving Before the First Tool Call
item.description: Fresh Pi, OpenClaw, and Codex changes point to a quieter layer of agent safety: decide what a project may load, what a gateway may carry, and what execution context a thread owns before the model starts acting.

item.title: Pi Coding Agent You Can Keep Rewriting
item.description: The Pi repo shows a different coding-agent bargain: a small terminal harness, a public maintainer culture, and a source-visible insistence that workflows should live in extensions, skills, packages, files, and external sandboxes instead of a swollen core.

item.title: Agent Automation Is Becoming Scheduled Operations
item.description: Fresh OpenClaw, Codex, and Hermes Agent commits show agent work moving beyond the foreground chat turn into scheduled jobs, background threads, heartbeat runs, and provenance-aware session rotation.

item.title: Agent Setup Is Becoming Runtime Onboarding
item.description: Fresh OpenClaw, Hermes Agent, and Codex commits show agent tools trying to erase the gap between "this capability exists" and "a user can actually run it."

item.title: Agent Control Is Moving Into the Live Interface
item.description: Fresh OpenClaw, Crush, Hermes Agent, and Codex commits show approvals, steering, and configuration errors moving from background policy into the moments where a human can actually change an agent run.

item.title: The Twelve-Parent Octopus That Made Git Blink
item.description: In January 2006, ACPI maintainer Len Brown folded eleven Linux topic branches into one twelve-parent merge commit. It looked almost comic, pushed Git's own tools to grow sharper eyes, and now reads like a warning label for the agent era: clean integration is not the same as clear accountability.

item.title: Agent Identity Is Becoming Runtime Infrastructure
item.description: Fresh Hermes Agent, OpenClaw, and Codex commits show profile, credential, and model-choice state moving out of setup screens and into live agent runtime controls.

item.title: Git Won Source Control. AI Agents Are Testing What a Commit Means
item.description: Linus Torvalds's first eleven-file commit made content-addressed history the durable core of software collaboration. Twenty-one years later, agents are multiplying branches, worktrees, commits, and pull requests - and forcing source management to answer who acted, why, and who is accountable.

item.title: Agent Events Are Getting Return Addresses
item.description: Fresh Hermes Agent, Crush, OpenClaw, and Codex commits show agent runtimes attaching correlation, provenance, and client identity to events so the right run, session, surface, or device receives them.

item.title: Google's Agent Center of Gravity Has Moved Beyond Gemini CLI
item.description: From an Apache-licensed terminal agent to the non-public shared harness behind Antigravity CLI, desktop, SDK, and Managed Agents, Google's I/O transition is an architecture and governance shift, not a rename.

item.title: Agent Failure States Are Becoming Instructions
item.description: Fresh LangChain, Crush, Gemini CLI, and OpenClaw commits show agent tools turning denial, fallback, routing, and policy drift into explicit next steps.

item.title: Rust's Bootstrap Chain Is a Trust Story, Not a Smoking Gun
item.description: The Rust repo and compiler docs confirm the Trusting Trust shape: modern rustc starts from an earlier rustc binary. The evidence supports an audit question, not an accusation.

item.title: Agent Work Is Getting a Back Button
item.description: Fresh Codex and Hermes commits show a practical shift in agent tooling: longer-running work now needs recovery rails, not just more controls.

item.title: Agent Policy Is Moving Into Delivered Layers
item.description: Fresh Codex and Hermes commits point to a new control-plane pressure: the rules an agent follows are starting to arrive as cloud bundles, layered requirements, and entitlement-aware tool setup instead of only local config choices.

item.title: Agent Tool Menus Are Becoming Runtime Infrastructure
item.description: Fresh Codex and Hermes commits show a quieter agent shift: the visible list of tools is becoming a runtime contract about what can be suggested, enabled, discovered, and checked without breaking the session.

item.title: Agent Control Settings Are Becoming Session State
item.description: Fresh Codex and Hermes commits show a quieter agent shift: permissions, tool behavior, lineage, approval context, and auth fallbacks are being treated as state that has to survive the next turn.

item.title: Agent Sandboxes Are Learning Their Cleanup Rules
item.description: Fresh Codex and Hermes commits point to a practical agent shift: the local machine is becoming part of the runtime contract, with workspace roots, filesystem denies, Docker persistence, orphan cleanup, and lifecycle commands written down in code.

item.title: Agent UI Is Becoming the Control Room
item.description: Fresh commits in Codex, Hermes, and Gemini CLI point to a practical agent shift: the human interface is turning into the place where long-running work gets linked, stopped, switched, and steered.

item.title: Agent Context Becomes Runtime State
item.description: Fresh commits in Codex, Gemini CLI, and Hermes point to a quieter agent shift: context is being stored, routed, measured, and traced instead of merely stuffed into prompts.

item.title: Agent Reliability Is Moving Into the Test Rig
item.description: The newest source-readable trail points away from model mystique and toward the workflows that test, trace, serialize, and maintain agent behavior.

item.title: Agent Runtimes Are Learning Where to Say No
item.description: After shipping more visible control planes, agent projects are now tightening the places where tools, plugins, credentials, chat context, and test runs are allowed to flow.

item.title: Agent Runtimes Are Learning to Ship Their Control Plane
item.description: A set of Codex, Hermes Agent, Gemini CLI, and Crush commits shows agent tools packaging the machinery around the model: shells, platform adapters, context profiles, and skill catalogs.

item.title: Agent Runtimes Are Making Their Limits Explicit
item.description: Recent Crush and LangChain changes show agent infrastructure turning hidden boundaries - shell permission, context overflow, and model token limits - into runtime contracts that users and developers can see.

item.title: Agent Goals Are Becoming Runtime State
item.description: Recent Codex and Hermes Agent changes show coding agents turning goals from chat commands into durable metadata, resumable session state, and queued work control.
